When 1,000 × ₹100 debentures are issued at ₹110 to a vendor: Vendor Dr. ₹1,10,000 → Debentures Cr. ₹1,00,000; Sec. Premium Cr. ₹10,000. No cash, yet Securities Premium is created. R correctly identifies that the trigger is the premium on issue price, not the cash nature of the transaction.

questions.question: Stone Ltd. issued 1,000, 12% Debentures of ₹1,000 each for cash: 400 at par, 300 at 5% premium, 300 at 8% discount. Total debenture interest for the year is:
questions.explanation: Interest is always on face value of ALL debentures regardless of issue price. Total face value = 1,000 × ₹1,000 = ₹10,00,000. Annual interest = 12% × ₹10,00,000 = ₹1,20,000. The issue price (par/premium/discount) is irrelevant to interest calculation. Interest for 400 at par = 12% × ₹4,00,000 = ₹48,000. For 300 at premium = 12% × ₹3,00,000 = ₹36,000. For 300 at discount = 12% × ₹3,00,000 = ₹36,000. Total = ₹1,20,000.

questions.question: Rapid Ltd. acquired a business paying consideration of ₹25,00,000 settled by: (i) cash ₹5,00,000; (ii) 8% Debentures ₹100 each at 10% discount for the balance. Net fair value of assets taken = ₹23,00,000. What is the Goodwill or Capital Reserve, and number of debentures issued?
questions.explanation: Purchase consideration ₹25,00,000 > Net assets ₹23,00,000 → Goodwill = ₹2,00,000. Balance settled by debentures = ₹25,00,000 − ₹5,00,000 = ₹20,00,000 (value given to vendor). Issue price per debenture = ₹100 − 10% = ₹90. Number of debentures = ₹20,00,000 ÷ ₹90 = 22,222 (approx). Face value = 22,222 × ₹100 = ₹22,22,200 (approx). Discount = ₹22,22,200 − ₹20,00,000 = ₹2,22,200. Goodwill = ₹2,00,000; approx 22,222 debentures.

questions.question: The Securities Premium Reserve balance can be used to issue bonus shares to existing shareholders. This is classified as:
options: Debit: Securities Premium Reserve; Credit: Bonus to Shareholders
Debit: Securities Premium Reserve; Credit: Share Capital
Debit: Retained Earnings; Credit: Share Capital
questions.explanation: When bonus shares are issued using Securities Premium Reserve: Securities Premium Reserve Dr. → Share Capital Cr. This converts reserves into paid-up capital. No cash flows — it is a purely book entry that increases Share Capital while reducing Securities Premium Reserve. It does NOT involve Bank (no cash) or a 'Bonus' account. Retained Earnings can also be used for bonus shares, but when Securities Premium is specifically used, it is debited to that account. This is a permitted use under Section 52(2).

questions.question: Keystone Ltd. issued 6,000, 10% Debentures of ₹100 each at par on 1st July 2023. Interest is payable on 31st December and 30th June every year. Books close 31st March. TDS @10%. What is the Outstanding Interest on Debentures shown in Balance Sheet on 31st March 2024?
questions.explanation: Annual interest = 10% × (6,000 × ₹100) = ₹60,000. Half-yearly = ₹30,000. Paid on 31st December 2023 (net of TDS). Next payment due 30th June 2024. At 31st March 2024: interest accrued for Jan–March (3 months) = ₹60,000 × 3/12 = ₹15,000. Wait — half-yearly cycle: 31st Dec and 30th June. At 31st March: 3 months of the second half-year (Jan–March) have passed but ₹30,000 for the full period (Jan–June) is not yet due. Accrued = ₹30,000 × 3/6 = ₹15,000. But if full half-year accrual is shown: ₹30,000. Standard exam approach: outstanding = interest accrued but not due = ₹15,000. Option D = ₹30,000 (full half-year outstanding — alternative approach).

questions.question: The rate of interest on debentures is determined by:
options: Board of Directors at each AGM
Fixed at the time of issue and stated on the face of the debenture
questions.explanation: Debenture interest rate is fixed at the time of issue and is stated on the debenture certificate/prospectus (e.g., '9% Debentures' means 9% p.a. fixed interest). Unlike dividend on equity shares (decided each year by the board), debenture interest is a contractual, fixed obligation set before issue. SEBI and RBI rates may influence market conditions but do not determine company-specific debenture rates. The board does not redecide interest at each AGM.

questions.question: Granite Ltd. issued 4,000, 9% Debentures of ₹250 each at a premium of 12% to a vendor for machinery. What is the value at which Machinery Account is debited?
questions.explanation: Issue price per debenture = ₹250 + 12% = ₹250 + ₹30 = ₹280. Value of consideration given = 4,000 × ₹280 = ₹11,20,000. Machinery Account is debited at consideration given = ₹11,20,000. Corresponding credits: 9% Debentures Cr. ₹10,00,000 (4,000 × ₹250 face value); Securities Premium Reserve Cr. ₹1,20,000 (4,000 × ₹30). Total Cr. = ₹11,20,000 = Machinery Dr. ✓

questions.question: Lambda Ltd. Balance Sheet shows: 8% Debentures ₹50,00,000; Securities Premium Reserve ₹8,00,000; Discount on Issue of Debentures ₹6,00,000. In the current year, company writes off ₹2,00,000 discount against P&L and ₹1,00,000 against Securities Premium. What are the closing balances?
questions.explanation: Opening Securities Premium = ₹8,00,000. Written off against SPR: ₹1,00,000. Closing SPR = ₹8,00,000 − ₹1,00,000 = ₹7,00,000. Opening Discount = ₹6,00,000. Written off (SPR) = ₹1,00,000. Written off (P&L) = ₹2,00,000. Total written off = ₹3,00,000. Closing Discount = ₹6,00,000 − ₹3,00,000 = ₹3,00,000. Closing: SPR ₹7,00,000; Discount on Issue ₹3,00,000. Option A (and B, same) is correct.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Debenture interest accrued but not paid is shown under Current Liabilities in the Balance Sheet.
Reason (R): Accrued expenses that are payable within 12 months are classified as current liabilities per Schedule III.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R explains A. Accrued interest on debentures is typically due within 12 months (the next interest payment date). Schedule III of the Companies Act, 2013, classifies liabilities payable within 12 months as Current Liabilities under 'Other Current Liabilities.' R correctly states the Schedule III criterion for current classification, which explains why accrued debenture interest (A) is a current liability. If somehow interest were not due for over 12 months, it could be non-current, but that is atypical.

questions.question: Magnet Ltd. issued 10,000, 10% Debentures of ₹100 each. 7,000 at ₹96 (cash); 3,000 at ₹104 (cash). Total discount and premium amounts, and net cash received:
When debentures are issued 'otherwise than for cash' (e.g., for services), the expense/asset recorded is:
options: At the lower of fair value of services and face value of debentures
At the fair value of consideration received (services/assets) = issue price of debentures
At the redemption value of debentures
questions.explanation: When debentures are issued for non-cash consideration, the asset or expense is recorded at the value of consideration given — which equals the issue price of debentures (number × issue price). This follows the cost concept. Whether it is services (Goodwill/Expense) or assets (machinery, land), the debit is at issue price × quantity. For example, 1,000 × ₹100 debentures at ₹95 issued for services: Services Dr. ₹95,000; Discount Dr. ₹5,000 → Debentures Cr. ₹1,00,000. Redemption value is irrelevant at issue stage.

questions.question: Pinnacle Ltd. purchased a running business for ₹15,00,000 (cash ₹3,00,000 + balance by 10% Debentures at 20% premium). Fair value of net assets = ₹13,00,000. What is the goodwill and face value of debentures issued?
questions.explanation: Purchase consideration > Net assets: Goodwill = ₹15,00,000 − ₹13,00,000 = ₹2,00,000. Debenture consideration = ₹15,00,000 − ₹3,00,000 = ₹12,00,000. Issue price per ₹100 debenture = ₹120. Face value of debentures = ₹12,00,000 ÷ ₹120 × ₹100 = ₹10,00,000. Number of debentures = ₹12,00,000 ÷ ₹120 = 10,000. Face value = 10,000 × ₹100 = ₹10,00,000. Securities Premium = 10,000 × ₹20 = ₹2,00,000. Option A: Goodwill ₹2,00,000; Face value ₹10,00,000.

questions.question: Which of the following does NOT correctly describe a journal entry related to interest on debentures?
options: On accrual: Debenture Interest Dr. → Outstanding Interest Payable Cr.
On payment of accrued interest: Outstanding Interest Payable Dr. → Bank Cr.
On transfer to P&L: P&L A/c Dr. → Debenture Interest A/c Cr.
On accrual: Bank Dr. → Debenture Interest Cr.
questions.explanation: Option D is incorrect. On accrual of interest, Bank is NOT debited — cash has not been received (interest is an expense, not income). Accrual entry: Debenture Interest Dr. → Outstanding Interest Payable Cr. Bank is only involved when cash is actually paid. Option D reverses the entry and incorrectly treats interest as income (crediting Debenture Interest while debiting Bank is an income receipt pattern, not an expense). Options A, B, and C are all correct entries in the interest accounting cycle.

questions.question: Pulse Ltd. issued 5,000, 10% Debentures of ₹100 each at a discount of 10%. What is the total amount of loss on issue to be disclosed as a fictitious asset in the Balance Sheet immediately after issue?
questions.explanation: Discount on Issue = 10% × (5,000 × ₹100) = 10% × ₹5,00,000 = ₹50,000. This entire ₹50,000 is shown as 'Discount on Issue of Debentures' (fictitious asset) in the Balance Sheet immediately after issue. It has not been written off yet. Issue price = ₹90; Bank Dr. = 5,000 × ₹90 = ₹4,50,000; Discount Dr. = ₹50,000; Debentures Cr. = ₹5,00,000. Balance Sheet: Debentures (liability) ₹5,00,000; Discount on Issue (asset) ₹50,000.

questions.question: Flash Ltd. issued 12,000, 9% Debentures of ₹50 each on 1st January 2024. 8,000 at ₹52 for cash; 4,000 at ₹48 to vendor for equipment. Books close 31st March. No write-off in first year. What figures appear in Balance Sheet 31st March 2024?
questions.explanation: Total debentures (face value) = 12,000 × ₹50 = ₹6,00,000. Cash issue (8,000 at ₹52): SPR = 8,000 × ₹2 = ₹16,000. Vendor issue (4,000 at ₹48): Discount = 4,000 × ₹2 = ₹8,000. Balance Sheet 31st March 2024 (no write-off): Debentures ₹6,00,000 (Non-Current Liability); Securities Premium Reserve ₹16,000 (Equity); Discount on Issue ₹8,000 (Other Non-Current Asset — fictitious). Option A is correct.

questions.question: The interest on debentures issued for consideration other than cash is treated as:
options: No interest is applicable on such debentures
Revenue expenditure charged to P&L like any other debenture interest
questions.explanation: Once debentures are issued — whether for cash or non-cash consideration — they are identical financial instruments. The interest obligation is the same regardless of how the debentures were originally issued. Interest on debentures issued for assets/services is treated as a revenue expenditure and charged to Statement of P&L as Finance Costs. The mode of issue has no bearing on interest treatment. It is NOT capital expenditure and is definitely NOT adjusted against the asset acquired.

questions.question: Spark Ltd. issued 7,500, 12% Debentures of ₹200 each: 3,000 at par (cash); 2,500 at 8% premium (cash); 2,000 at 6% discount (vendor for land). Annual interest = ₹1,80,000. What is the annual Debenture Interest attributable to discount-issued debentures?
questions.explanation: Interest is on face value of ALL debentures. Interest on 2,000 discount-issued debentures = 12% × (2,000 × ₹200) = 12% × ₹4,00,000 = ₹48,000. Verify total: 7,500 × ₹200 × 12% = ₹18,00,000 × 12% = ₹1,80,000. ✓ Interest does not depend on issue price — always face value × rate. The ₹48,000 is interest on those 2,000 debentures at face value, charged to P&L.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Debentures issued at a premium are always more attractive to investors than those issued at par.
Reason (R): Higher issue price (premium) reflects stronger market confidence in the company's creditworthiness.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: A is false — debentures at premium are LESS attractive to investors (they pay more upfront for the same face value and fixed return). Investors prefer discount issues where they pay less for the same return, resulting in a higher effective yield. R is true — premium pricing does reflect stronger creditworthiness and market confidence in the issuer. However, A incorrectly concludes that premium is more attractive to investors. Investor attractiveness relates to yield (return on investment), not market confidence alone.

questions.question: Brook Ltd. Balance Sheet shows Discount on Issue of Debentures ₹1,20,000. This was created 2 years ago from a 6-year debenture issue. How much has already been written off?
questions.explanation: Current balance = ₹1,20,000 (remaining). Total write-off period = 6 years; annual write-off = Total ÷ 6. After 2 years, balance = Total − (2 × Annual). Let Total = X. Remaining = X − 2(X/6) = X(1 − 1/3) = 2X/3 = ₹1,20,000 → X = ₹1,80,000. Annual write-off = ₹30,000. Written off = 2 × ₹30,000 = ₹60,000. Wait: balance after 2 years = ₹1,20,000 means 2/6 written off = 1/3 of total written off. 2/3 remaining = ₹1,20,000 → Total = ₹1,80,000. Written off = ₹60,000. Option A (₹40,000) is incorrect. Option D (₹60,000) is correct.

questions.question: Which of the following is included in Non-Current Liabilities in the Balance Sheet?
questions.explanation: 10% Debentures due after 3 years are classified as Non-Current Liabilities (Long-term Borrowings) since they are not payable within 12 months. Accrued interest is a Current Liability. TDS payable is a Current Liability. Discount on Issue is an asset (fictitious), not a liability at all. The principal amount of debentures with maturity beyond 12 months is always Non-Current Liability per Schedule III.

questions.question: Cyan Ltd. issued 9,000, 10% Debentures of ₹100 each on 1st April 2023 at a discount of 10%. Discount written off over 3 years. Securities Premium available = ₹15,000. Company uses SPR first. How much Discount on Issue appears in Balance Sheet at 31st March 2025?
questions.explanation: Total discount = 10% × ₹9,00,000 = ₹90,000. Annual write-off = ₹30,000. Year 1: use SPR ₹15,000 + P&L ₹15,000 = ₹30,000 written off. SPR exhausted. Year 2: P&L ₹30,000 written off. Total written off by 31st March 2025 (end Year 2) = ₹60,000. Balance = ₹90,000 − ₹60,000 = ₹30,000. Option B = ₹30,000.

questions.question: River Ltd.'s issued capital includes: Equity Share Capital ₹50,00,000; 10% Debentures ₹30,00,000 (face value, issued at 5% premium); Securities Premium Reserve ₹1,50,000 (from debenture issue). Discount on Issue of Debentures (from different tranche) ₹80,000. What is the total 'Reserves & Surplus' figure for Balance Sheet?
questions.explanation: Reserves & Surplus includes Securities Premium Reserve ₹1,50,000. The Discount on Issue of Debentures (₹80,000) is a fictitious ASSET — it is deducted from assets side, not from Reserves & Surplus. Debentures (₹30,00,000) are a Non-Current Liability, not part of Reserves & Surplus. Equity Share Capital is shown separately. Reserves & Surplus = Securities Premium Reserve ₹1,50,000 (only item given). Answer = ₹1,50,000. Option A.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): A company can issue debentures at different prices simultaneously — some at premium and some at discount — in the same offering.
Reason (R): Different debenture tranches can have different pricing based on market conditions, tenure, or security offered.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R explains A. A company can indeed issue different tranches of debentures at different prices in the same year (e.g., secured debentures at premium and unsecured at discount). R correctly identifies why: different tranches may have varying risk profiles, tenures, interest rates, or security, leading to different market-determined prices. NCERT examples frequently show companies issuing debentures at par, premium, and discount in the same problem. The accounting records each tranche separately.

questions.question: Amber Ltd. issued 2,000, 11% Debentures of ₹100 each at par for cash, and 1,000 debentures at ₹110 to a promoter for software services. What is the total entry to Debentures Account (credit)?
questions.explanation: Debentures Account is credited at face value for ALL debentures regardless of issue price. Total debentures = (2,000 + 1,000) = 3,000 × ₹100 = ₹3,00,000. For cash issue: Bank Dr. ₹2,00,000 → Debentures Cr. ₹2,00,000. For promoter issue: Services/Software Dr. ₹1,10,000 → Debentures Cr. ₹1,00,000; Securities Premium Cr. ₹10,000. Total Debentures Cr. = ₹2,00,000 + ₹1,00,000 = ₹3,00,000.

questions.question: Titan Metals Ltd. shows the following in its trial balance: 12% Debentures ₹40,00,000 (Cr.); Debenture Interest (Dr.) ₹4,80,000; Discount on Issue of Debentures ₹3,20,000 (Dr.); Securities Premium ₹2,00,000 (Cr.). For the year, annual write-off is ₹80,000. Company writes off against SPR first. After adjustments, P&L is debited for discount write-off by:
questions.explanation: SPR available = ₹2,00,000. Annual discount write-off = ₹80,000. Since ₹2,00,000 SPR > ₹80,000 write-off, the entire ₹80,000 is absorbed by SPR. No P&L charge for discount write-off. Entry: SPR Dr. ₹80,000 → Discount on Issue Cr. ₹80,000. P&L is charged ₹Nil for discount write-off. P&L is separately charged ₹4,80,000 for interest (already in trial balance). So P&L debit for discount = Nil. Option B.

questions.question: In the context of debenture issue, 'face value' is also known as:
questions.explanation: Face value = Nominal value = Par value — these terms are synonymous in accounting and finance. They refer to the stated value printed on the debenture certificate (e.g., ₹100, ₹500, ₹1,000). This is the base for interest calculation and the value at which the Debentures Account is credited. Issue price can be par/above/below face value. Redemption price is the amount paid at maturity. Market value fluctuates daily based on market conditions.

questions.question: Bolt Tech Ltd. issued 15,000, 9% Debentures of ₹100 each on 1st April 2023. 6,000 at ₹95 (cash); 5,000 at ₹103 (cash); 4,000 at ₹100 to vendor. Annual interest charge to P&L for year ended 31st March 2024 = ?
questions.explanation: Annual interest = 9% × (15,000 × ₹100) = 9% × ₹15,00,000 = ₹1,35,000. Interest is on face value of ALL debentures (15,000 × ₹100) regardless of issue price or consideration type. P&L charge = ₹1,35,000 for the full year. Options B and C incorrectly use issue prices. The issue price affects Bank/Discount/Premium accounts only, not the annual interest calculation.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of the journal entry for issuing debentures at discount for cash?
questions.explanation: Securities Premium Reserve (Credit) is NOT part of the entry for debentures issued at DISCOUNT. Securities Premium arises only when debentures are issued at PREMIUM. For discount issue: Bank Dr. (issue price) + Discount on Issue Dr. → Debentures Cr. (face value). There is no Securities Premium entry when issue price < face value. Including Securities Premium Credit in a discount entry would be a fundamental accounting error.

questions.question: Galaxy Ltd. purchased assets: Building ₹15,00,000 and Equipment ₹7,00,000 from Mars & Co. Settlement: ₹5,00,000 cash + 10% Debentures of ₹100 each at 15% discount for balance. How many debentures issued, and what is Discount on Issue?
questions.explanation: Purchase consideration = ₹15,00,000 + ₹7,00,000 − ₹0 (no liabilities assumed) = ₹22,00,000. Wait — we assume all assets, no liabilities. PC = ₹22,00,000. Cash = ₹5,00,000. Balance by debentures = ₹17,00,000. Issue price = ₹100 − 15% = ₹85. Number = ₹17,00,000 ÷ ₹85 = 20,000. Face value = 20,000 × ₹100 = ₹20,00,000. Discount = ₹20,00,000 − ₹17,00,000 = ₹3,00,000. Option A: 20,000 debentures; Discount ₹3,00,000.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): The annual write-off of Discount on Issue of Debentures reduces the Balance Sheet value of the fictitious asset progressively.
Reason (R): Each credit to Discount on Issue of Debentures reduces its debit balance until fully eliminated.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R is the precise accounting mechanism explaining A. The Discount on Issue account has a debit balance (it is an asset). Write-off entry: P&L/SPR Dr. → Discount on Issue Cr. Each credit reduces the debit balance. For example: Original balance ₹50,000; after first year credit ₹10,000 → balance ₹40,000; and so on until nil. This is exactly what R describes, making it the correct explanation of A.

questions.question: Why is Debenture Interest shown separately as a 'Finance Cost' in the Statement of P&L rather than included in 'Other Expenses'?
options: Because it is paid to insiders, not external parties
Because Schedule III requires specific classification of financing costs separately
Because it is only paid if profits are available
Because debentureholders are part-owners of the company
questions.explanation: Schedule III of the Companies Act, 2013, specifically prescribes that finance costs (including interest on borrowings such as debentures, bank loans, etc.) be shown as a separate line item in the Statement of P&L. This provides transparency to users about the cost of capital financing. It is distinct from operational costs (materials, employee costs) and other expenses. Finance costs reflect the cost of debt and are important for analysing financial leverage. Debentureholders are creditors, not owners.

questions.question: Polaris Ltd. shows outstanding Debenture Interest ₹36,000 in its opening Balance Sheet. During the year it pays ₹90,000 interest and accrues ₹45,000 at year-end. What is the Debenture Interest expense charged to P&L for the current year?
questions.explanation: P&L interest expense = Payments − Opening outstanding + Closing outstanding = ₹90,000 − ₹36,000 + ₹45,000 = ₹99,000. Alternatively: Interest expense = amount accrued for the period on an accrual basis. Opening ₹36,000 was already expensed in prior year. Current year expense = paid ₹90,000 (of which ₹36,000 relates to prior year + ₹54,000 current) + accrued ₹45,000 = ₹54,000 + ₹45,000 = ₹99,000. P&L charge = ₹99,000.

questions.question: Quartz Ltd. Balance Sheet (extract): 10% Debentures ₹20,00,000; Discount on Issue ₹1,60,000; Securities Premium ₹2,40,000. The debentures were issued 4 years ago with 5-year life. Discount being written off over 5 years. Securities Premium is intact. In the 5th year, the remaining discount is written off. What are the entries?
options: SPR Dr. ₹40,000 + P&L Dr. Nil → Discount Cr. ₹40,000
questions.explanation: Balance of Discount at start of Year 5 = ₹1,60,000. Total original discount over 5 years means ₹1,60,000 is the balance in year 4 (4 years gone, 1 remaining — so balance is 1/5 of original if written off equally). Annual write-off = total/5. If ₹1,60,000 is balance after 4 write-offs, then 1/5 of original = ₹40,000 → total = ₹2,00,000. 5th year write-off = ₹40,000. SPR = ₹2,40,000 (more than enough). Entry: SPR Dr. ₹40,000 → Discount on Issue Cr. ₹40,000. Option A is correct (P&L Dr. Nil; SPR Dr. ₹40,000).

questions.question: Debentures are included under which head in the Balance Sheet of a company?
questions.explanation: Debentures (long-term) are classified under Non-Current Liabilities → Long-term Borrowings in the Balance Sheet as per Schedule III of the Companies Act, 2013. They are borrowings (debt), not part of shareholders' funds (which is equity). They are non-current because repayment is typically beyond 12 months. If debentures were due within 12 months (current maturity), they would be classified as current liabilities, but by default debentures are non-current.

questions.question: Omega Ltd. purchased running business for ₹18,00,000. Net assets at fair value = ₹20,00,000. Settled by: ₹3,00,000 cash + 10% Debentures of ₹100 each at ₹125. How many debentures, and Capital Reserve?
questions.explanation: Purchase consideration = ₹18,00,000 < Net assets ₹20,00,000 → Capital Reserve = ₹2,00,000. Balance by debentures = ₹18,00,000 − ₹3,00,000 = ₹15,00,000. Issue price = ₹125. Number = ₹15,00,000 ÷ ₹125 = 12,000. Face value = 12,000 × ₹100 = ₹12,00,000. Securities Premium = 12,000 × ₹25 = ₹3,00,000. Option A: 12,000 debentures; Capital Reserve ₹2,00,000.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Issuing debentures at a discount increases the effective cost of borrowing for the company compared to a par issue.
Reason (R): The company receives less cash but pays interest on the full face value and repays face value at maturity, making the effective yield to investor (cost to company) higher than the coupon rate.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R provides the correct financial explanation for A. When debentures are issued at a discount: (1) Company receives less cash than face value, (2) pays interest on full face value throughout, and (3) repays full face value at maturity. So the investor earns more than the stated coupon rate — and equivalently, the company's cost of borrowing is higher than the coupon rate. R precisely explains this mechanism: less proceeds + same obligations = higher effective cost. This is the concept of Yield to Maturity (YTM) > Coupon Rate for discount bonds.

questions.question: Frost Ltd. issued 1,500, 10% Debentures of ₹1,000 each at par. Interest is paid half-yearly on 31st March and 30th September. On 31st March, TDS @ 10% is deducted. Bank paid on 31st March:
questions.explanation: Annual interest = 10% × (1,500 × ₹1,000) = ₹1,50,000. Half-yearly interest = ₹75,000. TDS on half-yearly = 10% × ₹75,000 = ₹7,500. Net Bank payment = ₹75,000 − ₹7,500 = ₹67,500. Entry: Debenture Interest Dr. ₹75,000 → Bank Cr. ₹67,500; TDS Payable Cr. ₹7,500. Bank paid = ₹67,500. Option D.

questions.question: Blaze Ltd. issued 5,000, 9% Debentures of ₹200 each. 2,000 at ₹190 (cash); 2,000 at ₹200 (cash); 1,000 at ₹210 to vendor for machinery. No write-offs yet. Balance Sheet impact:
options: Liabilities: Debentures ₹10,00,000; Assets: Discount ₹20,000; Machinery ₹2,10,000; SPR (equity) ₹10,000
questions.explanation: Total debentures (face) = 5,000 × ₹200 = ₹10,00,000 (liability). 2,000 at ₹190: Discount = 2,000 × ₹10 = ₹20,000 (asset). Cash received = 2,000 × ₹190 = ₹3,80,000. 2,000 at par: Cash = ₹4,00,000, no discount/premium. 1,000 at ₹210 (vendor): Machinery = 1,000 × ₹210 = ₹2,10,000 (asset); SPR = 1,000 × ₹10 = ₹10,000 (equity). Balance Sheet: Debentures ₹10,00,000; Discount ₹20,000; Machinery ₹2,10,000; SPR ₹10,000. Option A matches.

questions.question: The net effect of issuing debentures at par compared to issuing at a discount on the company's total assets is:
options: Same total assets in both cases
Higher total assets when issued at discount due to Discount on Issue
Lower total assets when issued at discount
questions.explanation: When debentures are issued at discount: Bank is debited at issue price (less than face) AND Discount on Issue is debited (fictitious asset equal to discount amount). Total assets debit = Bank + Discount = face value. For par issue: Bank debited at face value only. Both cases: total assets increase by face value (= total debenture credit). But for discount issue, total assets are the same (Bank at issue price + Discount = face value). Wait — technically same increase in total assets by face value in both cases. However, composition differs: par has more cash; discount has less cash but adds fictitious asset. Total assets increase = face value in both cases.

questions.question: Marble Ltd. shows in its Balance Sheet: 8% Debentures ₹25,00,000; Discount on Issue ₹1,00,000; Annual write-off ₹25,000. Debentures were issued 4 years ago. What is the remaining life of debentures?
questions.explanation: Remaining discount ₹1,00,000; annual write-off ₹25,000. Remaining years = ₹1,00,000 ÷ ₹25,000 = 4 years. Total life = 4 years (past) + 4 years (remaining) = 8 years. Alternatively, total discount = ₹25,000 × 8 = ₹2,00,000 (if uniform). Written off = 4 × ₹25,000 = ₹1,00,000 = ₹2,00,000 − ₹1,00,000. Remaining years of debenture life (and write-off) = ₹1,00,000 ÷ ₹25,000 = 4 years. Option A.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Interest on debentures is a charge against profits, not an appropriation of profits.
Reason (R): It must be paid before calculating taxable income and is not dependent on profits being available.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R correctly explains A. Charges against profits (like debenture interest, depreciation, taxes) are deducted BEFORE arriving at net profit. Appropriations (like dividends, transfers to reserves) happen AFTER profit determination. Debenture interest is a legal contractual obligation — it is paid regardless of whether profits exist, and it reduces taxable income. R correctly explains the distinction — being pre-profit (charge) vs post-profit (appropriation).

questions.question: Skyline Ltd. issued 10,000, 12% Debentures of ₹100 each at a premium of 8% on 1st January 2024. Year ends 31st March 2024. No previous SPR balance. Discount on Issue of ₹40,000 (from old debentures) is to be written off this year using new SPR. Remaining SPR balance after write-off?
questions.explanation: New Securities Premium = 8% × (10,000 × ₹100) = ₹80,000. Write off old Discount ₹40,000 against SPR: SPR Dr. ₹40,000 → Discount on Issue Cr. ₹40,000. Remaining SPR = ₹80,000 − ₹40,000 = ₹40,000. Option A = ₹40,000. Discount on Issue (old) is fully eliminated. The new SPR of ₹80,000 is partially used (₹40,000) and ₹40,000 remains.

questions.question: Diamond Ltd. issued 8,000, 10% Debentures of ₹100 each on 1st April 2023. 4,000 at ₹110 (cash); 4,000 at ₹90 (vendor for plant). On 31st March 2024: (a) full annual interest is paid; (b) discount written off against SPR. After all entries, the P&L is debited for debenture-related expenses by:
questions.explanation: Annual interest = 10% × (8,000 × ₹100) = ₹80,000 → charged to P&L. SPR from cash issue at ₹110: 4,000 × ₹10 = ₹40,000. Discount from vendor issue at ₹90: 4,000 × ₹10 = ₹40,000. Write-off: SPR Dr. ₹40,000 → Discount Cr. ₹40,000. SPR fully used. No P&L charge for discount. P&L debit = ₹80,000 (interest only). SPR absorbs entire discount. Option C is the correct description.

questions.question: Which of the following does NOT correctly describe the accounting treatment of Securities Premium Reserve from debenture issue?
options: It is part of Reserves & Surplus under Shareholders' Funds
It can be used to pay dividend to debentureholders
It can be used to issue fully paid bonus shares
It can be used to write off Discount on Issue of Debentures
questions.explanation: Paying dividend to debentureholders is NOT a permitted use of Securities Premium Reserve. Debentureholders receive interest (not dividends), and that interest is paid from current revenue — never from reserves. Furthermore, Section 52(2) of the Companies Act lists the only permitted uses: bonus share issue, writing off preliminary expenses, writing off discount on shares/debentures, and premium on redemption. Option B describes an incorrect use of SPR. Options A, C, and D are all correct treatments.

questions.question: Petal Ltd. issued 3,000, 8% Debentures of ₹100 each at a discount of 5% for cash. Discount on Issue is ₹15,000. After 3 years (out of 5-year life), discount balance remaining = ?
questions.explanation: Total discount = ₹15,000. Annual write-off = ₹15,000 ÷ 5 = ₹3,000. After 3 years, written off = 3 × ₹3,000 = ₹9,000. Remaining = ₹15,000 − ₹9,000 = ₹6,000. This ₹6,000 appears in Balance Sheet at the end of Year 3. Option B = ₹6,000.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about debenture interest is INCORRECT?
options: It is calculated on face value
It is a deductible expense for income tax purposes
It is payable only if the company earns profit
It is shown as Finance Costs in Statement of P&L
questions.explanation: Option C is incorrect. Debenture interest is payable regardless of whether the company earns profit — it is a fixed contractual obligation. This is a fundamental difference between debentures and equity shares (dividends). Options A, B, and D are all correct: interest is on face value; it is tax-deductible (reduces taxable income); it is shown as Finance Costs per Schedule III. The unconditional nature of debenture interest is what makes debentures riskier for the company than equity.

questions.question: Presto Ltd. Balance Sheet (extract): 10% Debentures ₹30,00,000; Interest outstanding ₹1,50,000. During current year interest paid = ₹3,00,000; Interest newly outstanding at year end = ₹75,000. Interest charged to P&L for the year =
questions.explanation: P&L interest = Cash paid − Opening outstanding + Closing outstanding = ₹3,00,000 − ₹1,50,000 + ₹75,000 = ₹2,25,000. This is the interest that relates to the current year. The ₹1,50,000 paid was from last year's outstanding (already expensed last year). Current year expense = ₹3,00,000 − ₹1,50,000 + ₹75,000 = ₹2,25,000. Option B.

questions.question: Zenon Ltd. purchased business for ₹35,00,000. Net assets = ₹38,00,000. Paid: ₹8,00,000 cash + 9% Debentures of ₹100 at 12.5% premium for balance. Find: (a) Capital Reserve, (b) Number of debentures, (c) Face value of debentures.

Assertion (A): Debentures issued to vendors at a discount result in the vendor receiving less than the face value of debentures as settlement.
Reason (R): The discount represents the difference between face value and the agreed settlement value — the vendor effectively accepts less.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R explains A. When a vendor accepts debentures at a discount (e.g., ₹90 debentures for ₹100 face value), the vendor receives debentures with a current market/agreed value of ₹90 each, settling the obligation at ₹90 per ₹100. The vendor effectively accepts less than face value (A). The discount (₹10) is the gap between face value and settlement value — the company records this as a loss (R). The connection between A and R is directly causal.

questions.question: Birch Ltd. issued 5,000, 11% Debentures of ₹100 each at 10% discount for acquiring a factory. What is the journal entry for acquiring the factory and issuing debentures?
options: Factory Dr. ₹5,00,000; Discount Dr. ₹50,000 → Debentures Cr. ₹5,50,000
Factory Dr. ₹4,50,000; Discount Dr. ₹50,000 → Debentures Cr. ₹5,00,000
Factory Dr. ₹5,00,000 → Debentures Cr. ₹4,50,000; Discount Cr. ₹50,000
Factory Dr. ₹5,50,000 → Debentures Cr. ₹5,00,000; SPR Cr. ₹50,000
questions.explanation: Issue price = ₹90 per debenture (10% discount on ₹100). Vendor receives debentures worth ₹4,50,000 (5,000 × ₹90). Factory recorded at consideration = ₹4,50,000. Discount on Issue = 5,000 × ₹10 = ₹50,000. Entry: Factory Dr. ₹4,50,000; Discount on Issue Dr. ₹50,000 → 11% Debentures Cr. ₹5,00,000 (face value). Option B is correct. Debentures are always credited at face value; asset at consideration (issue price); discount bridges the gap.

questions.question: Sigma Ltd.'s Books show: 10% Debentures ₹40,00,000; Interest payable 31st March and 30th September; Year closes 30th June. Interest paid on 31st March. Outstanding interest on 30th June (year-end):
questions.explanation: Annual interest = 10% × ₹40,00,000 = ₹4,00,000. Half-yearly = ₹2,00,000. Interest is payable 31st March and 30th September. Year closes 30th June. Interest for April–June (3 months, post-31st March payment) has accrued but next payment is 30th September. Outstanding = ₹4,00,000 × 3/12 = ₹1,00,000. Wait — half-yearly cycle: payment 31st March (Oct–March = 6 months) and 30th September (April–Sept = 6 months). At 30th June (year-end): 3 months of April-June interest is outstanding. Outstanding = ₹2,00,000 × 3/6 = ₹1,00,000. Option C = ₹1,00,000.

questions.question: When the Discount on Issue of Debentures is written off, which principle governs the time period over which it should be written off?
options: It must be written off entirely in the year of issue
It should be written off over the life of the debentures
It is written off when the company earns sufficient profits
No specific rule — it is at the complete discretion of management
questions.explanation: The Matching Principle and the principle of prudence govern the write-off of Discount on Issue. The discount represents the cost of raising long-term capital, so it should be matched against the period over which the benefit (use of borrowed funds) is received — i.e., the life of the debentures. Writing it off all in one year would create a disproportionately large charge. While management has discretion in method (straight-line is most common), the period must logically be the debenture life, not arbitrary.

questions.question: Pacific Ltd. issued 8,000, 9% Debentures of ₹500 each on 1st October 2022. 5,000 at ₹490 (cash); 3,000 at ₹510 (vendor for equipment). Year closes 31st March 2024 (second year). No write-offs in Year 1. In Year 2, discount written off equally over 10-year life. What is Discount on Issue balance in B/S at 31st March 2024?
questions.explanation: Discount from 5,000 cash debentures at ₹490: Discount = 5,000 × ₹10 = ₹50,000. Vendor issue at ₹510 → premium = 3,000 × ₹10 = ₹30,000 (to SPR). No discount from vendor issue. Total Discount on Issue = ₹50,000. Annual write-off = ₹50,000 ÷ 10 = ₹5,000. In Year 1 (Oct 2022–Mar 2023): no write-off (as stated). In Year 2 (Apr 2023–Mar 2024): ₹5,000 written off. Balance = ₹50,000 − ₹5,000 = ₹45,000. Option C.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): In the Balance Sheet, 'Discount on Issue of Debentures' is shown as a separate item under 'Other Non-Current Assets'.
Reason (R): It is an intangible asset with future economic benefit as it represents prepaid financing cost that benefits future periods.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: A is true — Discount on Issue is shown on the asset side (under Other Non-Current Assets/Miscellaneous Expenditure) of the Balance Sheet. However, R is false — it is NOT an intangible asset with future economic benefit. It has no realisable value, cannot be used in operations, and represents a PAST loss, not a future benefit. It is correctly called a 'fictitious asset' or 'deferred capital loss.' The asset classification is a bookkeeping convenience until it is written off — not because it creates economic benefit.

questions.question: Cedar Ltd. issued 4,000, 10% Debentures of ₹200 each at par. Interest is payable on 1st July and 1st January annually. Company closes accounts on 31st March. Outstanding interest at 31st March = ?
questions.explanation: Annual interest = 10% × (4,000 × ₹200) = ₹80,000. Half-yearly = ₹40,000 (payable 1st July and 1st January). At 31st March: last payment was 1st January (Jan for July–Dec period). Next payment: 1st July. Outstanding period: January 1 to March 31 = 3 months. Outstanding interest = ₹80,000 × 3/12 = ₹20,000. This is accrued for 3 months (Jan–March) not yet paid. Option C = ₹20,000.

questions.question: What does '12% Debentures ₹100' indicate on the face of a debenture certificate?
options: The debenture will be redeemed at ₹112
The annual interest is ₹12 per debenture (12% of ₹100 face value)
The market rate of interest is 12%
questions.explanation: 12% Debentures ₹100 means: face value = ₹100 per debenture; annual interest rate = 12%; annual interest per debenture = 12% × ₹100 = ₹12. The '12%' is the coupon rate applied to the face value of ₹100. It does NOT indicate: (a) redemption price (could be at par, premium, or discount), (b) issue price (could differ), or (c) market rate (which fluctuates). This is the fundamental interpretation of debenture notation.

questions.question: Quartz Ltd. issued 10,000, 10% Debentures of ₹100 each on 1st April 2023. 6,000 at ₹95; 4,000 at ₹105. All for cash. After 2 years' write-off (5-year discount period), Securities Premium = ₹20,000 (pre-existing). Company uses SPR first, then P&L. Remaining discount balance 31st March 2025?
questions.explanation: Discount = 6,000 × ₹5 = ₹30,000. SPR from new issue = 4,000 × ₹5 = ₹20,000. Pre-existing SPR = ₹20,000. Total SPR = ₹40,000. Annual write-off = ₹30,000 ÷ 5 = ₹6,000. Year 1: SPR Dr. ₹6,000 → Discount Cr. ₹6,000. Year 2: SPR Dr. ₹6,000. Total SPR used = ₹12,000. SPR remaining = ₹28,000. Discount written off = ₹12,000. Remaining discount = ₹30,000 − ₹12,000 = ₹18,000. Option A = ₹18,000.

questions.question: Rolex Enterprises issued 10,000, 9% Debentures of ₹100 each. Terms: 4,000 issued at par for cash; 3,000 at 10% premium to the public for cash; 3,000 at 5% discount to vendor for plant. Write-off discount immediately against available premium. Net P&L charge for Year 1 (full year interest + remaining discount after write-off)?
questions.explanation: Annual interest = 9% × ₹10,00,000 = ₹90,000 (on face value of all 10,000 debentures). SPR from premium issue: 3,000 × ₹10 = ₹30,000. Discount from vendor issue: 3,000 × ₹5 = ₹15,000. Write-off ₹15,000 discount against SPR: SPR Dr. ₹15,000 → Discount Cr. ₹15,000. SPR remaining = ₹30,000 − ₹15,000 = ₹15,000. Discount fully eliminated. P&L charge = ₹90,000 interest only. No discount charge to P&L. Option C.

questions.question: When 'consideration other than cash' involves debentures issued to acquire a business, the first step in accounting is:
options: Open a 'Business Purchase' account and credit it for purchase consideration
Open a 'Business Purchase' account, debit it for purchase consideration, and credit vendor
Record only the assets taken over
questions.explanation: The standard procedure for business purchase under consideration other than cash: Step 1: Business Purchase A/c Dr. (purchase consideration) → Vendor/Seller A/c Cr. (obligation to pay). Step 2: Record assets/liabilities taken over against Business Purchase A/c. Step 3: Settle vendor by issuing debentures → Vendor Dr. → Debentures Cr. (+ SPR if at premium). Option C correctly describes Step 1 — Business Purchase is debited (creating the obligation), and vendor account is credited (liability to pay). This is the standard exam procedure.

questions.question: Amber Ltd. acquired plant ₹3,60,000 and settled by issuing 10% Debentures of ₹100 at ₹120. The company also has 2,000 such debentures already issued at par for cash (₹2,00,000). Annual interest on ALL debentures (total) for the year = ?
questions.explanation: Debentures for plant: ₹3,60,000 ÷ ₹120 = 3,000 debentures. Face value = 3,000 × ₹100 = ₹3,00,000. Existing debentures (cash) = 2,000 × ₹100 = ₹2,00,000. Total face value = ₹3,00,000 + ₹2,00,000 = ₹5,00,000. Annual interest = 10% × ₹5,00,000 = ₹50,000. Option B. The plant debentures at ₹120 issue price still carry interest on ₹100 face value. Total annual interest = ₹50,000.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Debenture holders have priority over equity shareholders in receiving payment in the event of company liquidation.
Reason (R): Debentures represent secured or unsecured debt — creditors are paid before shareholders from the assets of the company.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R correctly explains A. In liquidation, the priority order is: secured creditors → unsecured creditors (including debentureholders) → preference shareholders → equity shareholders. Since debentureholders are creditors (not owners), they have a legal claim on the company's assets before shareholders receive anything. R correctly identifies that this priority exists because debentures represent debt (creditor relationship), not ownership. Secured debentures get even higher priority with their specific charge on assets.

questions.question: Crest Ltd.'s accounting equation after a debenture issue shows: Assets increased by ₹8,50,000 (Bank ₹7,65,000 + Discount ₹85,000). By how much and at what rate was the discount on debentures of face value ₹8,50,000?
questions.explanation: Face value = ₹8,50,000 (from accounting equation — total assets increase = face value). Discount = ₹85,000 (given). Discount rate = ₹85,000 ÷ ₹8,50,000 × 100 = 10%. Bank received = ₹7,65,000 = ₹8,50,000 − ₹85,000. Verification: ₹7,65,000 + ₹85,000 = ₹8,50,000. Discount = ₹85,000; Rate = 10%. Option B is correct.

questions.question: A company issues 'Convertible Debentures.' The interest on these debentures is treated as:
options: Part of share capital post conversion
Revenue expenditure (Finance Cost) charged to P&L until conversion
questions.explanation: Convertible debentures carry the same interest obligation as non-convertible ones until the conversion date. Interest is charged to P&L as Finance Cost (revenue expenditure) for every period the debentures remain outstanding. Upon conversion, the debentures are cancelled and shares are issued — but until then, the interest treatment is identical to any other debenture. There is no deferral or capitalization of interest on convertible debentures in standard accounting.

questions.question: Cascade Ltd. issued 20,000, 11% Debentures of ₹50 each on 1st April 2022 at ₹47 (discount ₹3 each). Discount written off equally over debenture life of 6 years. By 31st March 2026 (Year 4), what is the cumulative amount of discount written off?
questions.explanation: Total discount = 20,000 × ₹3 = ₹60,000. Annual write-off = ₹60,000 ÷ 6 = ₹10,000. By 31st March 2026 = 4 years (FY 2022-23, 2023-24, 2024-25, 2025-26). Cumulative write-off = 4 × ₹10,000 = ₹40,000. Remaining balance = ₹60,000 − ₹40,000 = ₹20,000. Option A = ₹40,000 (cumulative written off).

questions.question: Zenith Infrastructure Ltd. issued 15,000, 10% Debentures: 8,000 at ₹96 (cash); 5,000 at ₹100 (cash); 2,000 at ₹108 (vendor for crane). Immediately writes off discount using available premium. What is balance of Discount on Issue post write-off?
questions.explanation: Discount: 8,000 × ₹4 = ₹32,000. Premium (from vendor debentures at ₹108): 2,000 × ₹8 = ₹16,000. Write-off: SPR Dr. ₹16,000 → Discount Cr. ₹16,000. Remaining discount = ₹32,000 − ₹16,000 = ₹16,000. Balance of Discount on Issue = ₹16,000. Option A = ₹16,000. Option B (Nil) is incorrect — premium (₹16,000) can only absorb ₹16,000 of the ₹32,000 discount.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): When a company acquires net assets of ₹10,00,000 by issuing debentures worth ₹12,00,000 (face value) at a discount of ₹2,00,000 to the vendor, the vendor's account is credited with ₹10,00,000.
Reason (R): The vendor account records only the purchase consideration (agreed price), not the face value of debentures.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R explains A. The vendor (seller) receives debentures at issue price (₹10,00,000 consideration), not at face value (₹12,00,000). The vendor account is credited with the purchase consideration = ₹10,00,000. Face value of debentures = ₹12,00,000 (higher). The discount ₹2,00,000 is recorded as Discount on Issue. Entry: Vendor Cr. ₹10,00,000 (obligation to pay); Then: Vendor Dr. ₹10,00,000; Discount Dr. ₹2,00,000 → Debentures Cr. ₹12,00,000. R correctly identifies that vendor account = purchase consideration.

questions.question: Dune Ltd. issued ₹5,00,000 worth of 10% Debentures at a premium of 10%. Total debentures in the market have face value of ₹5,00,000. Annual interest payable to debentureholders =
questions.explanation: Interest is always on face value = ₹5,00,000. Annual interest = 10% × ₹5,00,000 = ₹50,000. The premium (₹50,000 cash received extra) goes to Securities Premium Reserve — it does not increase the debenture liability or affect interest calculation. Interest is always on face value regardless of issue price. Option B = ₹50,000.

questions.question: Polar Ltd. issued 5,000, 9% Debentures of ₹100 each on 1st April 2023 at 6% discount for cash. Year closes 31st March. Interest payable on 30th September and 31st March. TDS 10%. For year ended 31st March 2024: (a) Total cash paid for interest; (b) TDS payable on 31st March.
questions.explanation: Annual interest = 9% × ₹5,00,000 = ₹45,000. Half-yearly = ₹22,500. Total paid for full year = ₹45,000. TDS on each payment = 10% × ₹22,500 = ₹2,250. Net per payment = ₹20,250. Total cash paid for year = 2 × ₹20,250 = ₹40,500. TDS paid in September = ₹2,250. TDS payable on 31st March = ₹2,250. Option A: Cash paid ₹40,500; TDS (on March payment) ₹2,250 — but total TDS for year = ₹4,500. If question asks total TDS: ₹4,500. If 31st March TDS only: ₹2,250. Option A shows ₹40,500 and ₹4,500 (total TDS). This is A.

questions.question: When a company issues debentures at a discount, the company's working capital is affected as follows compared to par issue:
options: Working capital increases by the discount amount
Working capital is lower by the discount amount (less cash received)
Working capital decreases by the face value
questions.explanation: Working capital (Current Assets − Current Liabilities) is affected by the cash received. For par issue: Bank increases by face value. For discount issue: Bank increases by only the issue price (face − discount). So compared to par issue, less cash is received → lower current assets → lower working capital by exactly the discount amount. The debenture liability is Non-Current (same face value in both cases), so it doesn't affect working capital directly. The shortfall in cash = discount = reduction in working capital vs par issue.

questions.question: Flash Ltd. issued 6,000, 8% Debentures of ₹500 each. 2,000 at ₹480; 2,000 at ₹500; 2,000 at ₹520 (all for cash). Total discount, total premium, and total cash received:
options: Discount ₹40,000; Premium ₹40,000; Cash ₹30,00,000 — same as A
questions.explanation: 2,000 at ₹480: Cash = ₹9,60,000; Discount = 2,000 × ₹20 = ₹40,000. 2,000 at ₹500: Cash = ₹10,00,000; No discount/premium. 2,000 at ₹520: Cash = ₹10,40,000; Premium = 2,000 × ₹20 = ₹40,000. Total cash = ₹9,60,000 + ₹10,00,000 + ₹10,40,000 = ₹30,00,000. Discount = ₹40,000; Premium = ₹40,000. Option A and C show same values — both correct. Answer = A (Cash ₹30,00,000; Discount ₹40,000; Premium ₹40,000).

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Interest on debentures issued during the year for only part of the year is accrued on a time-proportion basis.
Reason (R): The accrual concept requires that expenses be recognized in the period to which they relate, regardless of when payment is made.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R explains A. If debentures are issued mid-year (e.g., 1st October), only 6 months' interest applies to that financial year. The accrual concept (R) requires recognizing the interest expense in the period it is earned/owed — not when paid. So 6 months' accrual is correct (A). R is the precise accounting principle (accrual concept / matching principle) that mandates time-proportion accrual for mid-year issues.

questions.question: Sunbeam Ltd. issued 25,000, 8% Debentures of ₹100 each: 10,000 at par (cash); 10,000 at 8% premium (cash); 5,000 at 4% discount (vendor). Full year interest = ₹2,00,000. What is the Securities Premium Reserve balance after write-off of discount against it?
questions.explanation: SPR from premium issue: 10,000 × ₹8 = ₹80,000. Discount from vendor issue: 5,000 × ₹4 = ₹20,000. Write-off: SPR Dr. ₹20,000 → Discount Cr. ₹20,000. Net SPR = ₹80,000 − ₹20,000 = ₹60,000. Discount fully eliminated. Annual interest = 8% × 25,000 × ₹100 = ₹2,00,000 ✓. SPR balance = ₹60,000. Option A.

questions.question: Lava Ltd. had the following debenture-related transactions: (1) Issued 5,000 × ₹100 debentures at ₹92 for cash; (2) Issued 3,000 × ₹100 debentures at ₹108 to vendor for machinery; (3) Issued 2,000 × ₹100 debentures at par to promoter for services. What is total Debentureholders' interest obligation annually at 10%?
questions.explanation: Total debentures = (5,000 + 3,000 + 2,000) = 10,000 × ₹100 face value = ₹10,00,000. Annual interest = 10% × ₹10,00,000 = ₹1,00,000. The issue prices (₹92, ₹108, ₹100) have NO effect on the interest obligation — always calculated on face value. Option B = ₹1,00,000.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT part of the complete accounting for debentures issued at 10% premium for non-cash (asset acquisition)?
options: Asset Account Dr. at issue price
Debentures Account Cr. at face value
Securities Premium Reserve Cr. at premium amount
Bank Account Dr. at issue price
questions.explanation: When debentures are issued for consideration other than cash (asset acquisition), no Bank/Cash entry is passed. Option D (Bank Dr.) would only appear in a cash transaction. The correct entries are: Asset Dr. (at consideration/issue price) → Debentures Cr. (face value) + Securities Premium Cr. (premium). Bank is not involved. Options A, B, and C are all correct entries for non-cash premium debenture issue.

questions.question: Titan Ltd. issued 8,000, 12% Debentures of ₹100 each at ₹88 for cash on 1st July 2023. Year closes 31st March 2024 (9 months). Discount to be written off over 6 years (annual installment basis from 31st March 2024). P&L charge for the year for: (a) interest; (b) discount write-off:
questions.explanation: Annual interest = 12% × ₹8,00,000 = ₹96,000. For 9 months (July 2023–March 2024): Interest = ₹96,000 × 9/12 = ₹72,000. Total discount = 12% discount (₹100 − ₹88 = ₹12) × 8,000 = ₹96,000. Annual write-off = ₹96,000 ÷ 6 = ₹16,000. First annual installment from 31st March 2024 = ₹16,000 (full annual installment, not apportioned). P&L: Interest ₹72,000 + Discount ₹16,000. Option A.

questions.question: A company has 10% Debentures on which interest is due but unpaid. On the Balance Sheet date, this is recorded as:
questions.explanation: Outstanding (accrued but unpaid) debenture interest is a current liability because it is an amount owed to debentureholders and is expected to be paid within the next 12 months. Per Schedule III, it is shown under 'Other Current Liabilities.' 'Income received in advance' is for revenue received but not yet earned — inapplicable here. 'Provision' implies uncertainty — accrued interest is certain. 'Deferred revenue expenditure' relates to deferred costs, not liabilities.

questions.question: Crystal Waters Ltd. issued 12,000, 9% Debentures of ₹250 each. 5,000 at ₹240 (cash); 4,000 at ₹250 (cash); 3,000 at ₹265 (vendor for pipeline). Discount to be written off against Securities Premium only. After write-off, remaining SPR balance?
questions.explanation: Discount: 5,000 × ₹10 = ₹50,000. SPR from vendor debentures: 3,000 × ₹15 = ₹45,000. Write off: SPR ₹45,000 used → Discount reduced by ₹45,000. Remaining discount = ₹50,000 − ₹45,000 = ₹5,000 (still as fictitious asset). Remaining SPR = ₹0 (all used). Wait — question asks SPR balance: ₹0 after write-off. But ₹5,000 discount remains. So SPR = ₹0 and remaining Discount = ₹5,000. None of the options show SPR = 0. Re-reading: SPR ₹45,000; Discount ₹50,000. After using all SPR: Remaining SPR = ₹0; Remaining Discount = ₹5,000. Option D ₹5,000 is the remaining discount, not SPR.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Debentures issued at a premium create a capital reserve that strengthens the company's Balance Sheet.
Reason (R): Securities Premium Reserve is a capital reserve that cannot be distributed as dividend, providing a permanent cushion to the company's net worth.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true but R does not fully explain A. A says premium 'creates a capital reserve' — technically Securities Premium Reserve is indeed a form of capital reserve. R correctly says it cannot be distributed as dividend and strengthens net worth. However, the precise reason A is true is that the premium increases the equity/net worth of the company (strengthens Balance Sheet). R focuses on the restriction on distribution rather than the Balance Sheet strengthening mechanism, making them both true but R an incomplete/non-direct explanation of A.

questions.question: Marble Tech Ltd. issued 4,000, 10% Debentures of ₹250 each at par for cash. What is the maximum possible interest deductible as finance cost for tax purposes in the year of issue (full year)?
questions.explanation: Annual interest = 10% × (4,000 × ₹250) = 10% × ₹10,00,000 = ₹1,00,000. This full ₹1,00,000 is deductible as finance cost for corporate tax purposes (subject to tax laws). For accounting purposes, ₹1,00,000 is charged to P&L as Finance Cost. The full annual interest is the maximum deductible amount for a full year of issue at par. Option A.

questions.question: Sun Corp issued 6,000, 10% Debentures of ₹100 each at ₹95 for cash on 1st April 2020. Life: 5 years (due 31st March 2025). Annual discount write-off. Current year = 2024-25 (final year). Discount Account balance at start of year = ₹6,000 (i.e., last installment). Which entry is correct?
options: P&L Dr. ₹6,000 → Discount on Issue Cr. ₹6,000
Discount on Issue Dr. ₹6,000 → P&L Cr. ₹6,000
questions.explanation: In the final year, the last installment of Discount on Issue is written off. Since Securities Premium was not mentioned (assume not available or already used), it is charged to P&L. Entry: P&L A/c Dr. ₹6,000 → Discount on Issue of Debentures A/c Cr. ₹6,000. This eliminates the fictitious asset and charges the final installment to profit. After this entry, Discount on Issue = nil. Option A is correct.

questions.question: Under Schedule III of Companies Act 2013, 'Discount on Issue of Debentures' appearing in the Balance Sheet is classified under:
options: Other Non-Current Assets (Miscellaneous Expenditure not written off)
questions.explanation: As per Schedule III, Discount on Issue of Debentures is a deferred capital loss classified under 'Other Non-Current Assets' (specifically under 'Miscellaneous Expenditure to the extent not written off or adjusted'). It is non-current because it is amortized over the debenture life (typically more than 1 year). It is an asset (debit balance) — not a liability or equity item. It has no realizable value — hence 'fictitious' — but appears as an asset until fully written off.

questions.question: Neutron Ltd. issued 10,000, 9% Debentures of ₹100 each at ₹104 for cash on 1st April 2023. What is the total Securities Premium Reserve shown in Shareholders' Equity after 2 years (31st March 2025), assuming no other entries?
questions.explanation: Securities Premium Reserve from issue = 10,000 × ₹4 (premium) = ₹40,000. Securities Premium Reserve is NOT amortized or reduced over time (unlike Discount on Issue). It remains at ₹40,000 after 2 years (or any years) unless specifically used for permitted purposes (bonus shares, writing off discount, etc.). Since no other entries are mentioned, SPR = ₹40,000 at 31st March 2025. Option A.

questions.question: Phoenix Ltd. took over the following from Rajan Co.: Land ₹10,00,000; Building ₹8,00,000; Plant ₹6,00,000; Debtors ₹3,00,000; Creditors (assumed) ₹2,00,000. Purchase price = ₹27,00,000. Settled: ₹5,00,000 cash + 9% Debentures of ₹100 each at ₹90. Find: Goodwill/Capital Reserve, No. of debentures, Face Value.

Assertion (A): Debentures issued as consideration for business acquisition are valued at the agreed purchase price (consideration), not at the face value of debentures.
Reason (R): Assets acquired should be recorded at cost, which is the value of consideration exchanged.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R explains A. The debentures are issued at an agreed issue price (which may be at premium or discount to face value). The assets/business acquired is recorded at the consideration value (issue price × number of debentures). R cites the cost principle — assets must be recorded at the cost incurred to acquire them. A correctly states that debentures are valued at the agreed purchase consideration (not face value) for asset recording purposes. The face value is only used for the Debentures Account credit.

questions.question: Axis Ltd. has Discount on Issue ₹2,40,000 and SPR ₹1,80,000. They write off discount: first SPR, then P&L equally over 4 remaining years. What is the P&L charge per year for discount?
questions.explanation: SPR available = ₹1,80,000. Total discount = ₹2,40,000. Remaining discount after using all SPR = ₹2,40,000 − ₹1,80,000 = ₹60,000. Annual write-off from P&L = ₹60,000 ÷ 4 = ₹15,000. The entire SPR is used first (either in one go or spread over first years). Once SPR is exhausted, ₹60,000 is charged to P&L over 4 years at ₹15,000/year. Option B.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an accurate statement about issue of debentures for consideration other than cash at premium?
options: Asset is debited at issue price (consideration given to vendor)
Vendor account is credited at purchase consideration
Bank is debited at issue price since debentures attract premium
Securities Premium is credited at the premium amount
questions.explanation: Option C is incorrect. In a non-cash debenture issue, NO bank entry is made — there is no cash transaction. The phrase 'Bank is debited at issue price since debentures attract premium' is completely wrong for a non-cash issue. Premium increases Securities Premium (a reserve), not Bank. Bank is only debited in cash issues. Options A, B, and D are all correct for non-cash premium debenture issues. This is a common conceptual trap in exams.

questions.question: Radium Ltd. issued 12,000, 10% Debentures of ₹100 each: 5,000 at ₹95; 4,000 at ₹100; 3,000 at ₹110 — all for cash. Annual interest = ₹1,20,000. Net cash received and balance of Discount on Issue (after applying SPR to offset discount):
options: Cash ₹11,75,000; Net Discount ₹Nil — same as A

questions.question: The accounting entry for 'Accrual of interest on debentures' that has not yet been paid is best described as:
options: Creating a liability and recognizing an expense simultaneously
questions.explanation: Accrual of interest creates: (1) An expense in P&L (Debenture Interest Dr.) — recognizing the cost for the period, and (2) A liability (Outstanding Interest Payable Cr.) — representing the obligation to pay in the future. This is the fundamental nature of accrual accounting — recognizing economic events when they occur, not when cash changes hands. It simultaneously increases expenses (reducing profit) and increases liabilities (obligation to debentureholders). Neither Bank nor face value is affected.

questions.question: Pinnacle Heights Ltd. issued 15,000, 9% Debentures of ₹200 each at a discount of 8% for acquisition of assets worth ₹27,60,000. What is the number of debentures, Discount on Issue, and Securities Premium (if any)?
questions.explanation: Face value per debenture = ₹200. Issue price = ₹200 − 8% = ₹184. Assets worth ₹27,60,000 = consideration = 15,000 × ₹184. Check: 15,000 × ₹184 = ₹27,60,000. ✓ Number = 15,000. Discount = 15,000 × ₹16 = ₹2,40,000. Face value = 15,000 × ₹200 = ₹30,00,000. No premium (issued at discount). SPR = Nil. Discount on Issue = ₹2,40,000. Option A.

questions.question: Spark Energy Ltd. issued 20,000, 11% Debentures of ₹100 each on 1st April 2022. Terms: 8,000 at ₹95 (public/cash); 7,000 at ₹100 (institutional/cash); 5,000 at ₹105 (promoter/non-cash for patent). For year 2022-23: (a) Interest (full year); (b) Securities Premium; (c) Discount on Issue:
options: Interest ₹2,20,000; SPR ₹25,000; Discount ₹40,000 — same as A
questions.explanation: Annual interest = 11% × (20,000 × ₹100) = 11% × ₹20,00,000 = ₹2,20,000. SPR from promoter debentures at ₹105: 5,000 × ₹5 = ₹25,000. Discount from public debentures at ₹95: 8,000 × ₹5 = ₹40,000. Interest = ₹2,20,000 (full year, all 20,000 debentures). Options A and C both show ₹2,20,000 — Option A and Option C are same. But Option B shows ₹2,00,000 which is incorrect. Correct answer: Interest ₹2,20,000; SPR ₹25,000; Discount ₹40,000 = Option A (and C).

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Goodwill arising from business acquisition is NOT written off against Securities Premium Reserve.
Reason (R): Section 52(2) of the Companies Act, 2013 lists only specific permitted uses of Securities Premium, and writing off Goodwill is not among them.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R explains A. Section 52(2) specifically limits the uses of Securities Premium Reserve to: (i) issue of fully-paid bonus shares, (ii) writing off preliminary expenses, (iii) writing off discount on issue of shares/debentures, and (iv) providing premium on redemption. Writing off Goodwill is NOT in this list. Therefore, Goodwill cannot be offset against Securities Premium — it is written off against P&L. R provides the precise statutory justification for A.

questions.question: Orion Chemicals issued 18,000, 10% Debentures of ₹100 each: 6,000 at ₹90 for cash; 8,000 at ₹100 for cash; 4,000 at ₹115 to vendor for chemical plant. After writing off discount (if any) against premium (if any), net entries in Balance Sheet:
options: Debentures ₹18,00,000; SPR ₹30,000; Discount Nil — same as A
questions.explanation: SPR: 4,000 × ₹15 = ₹60,000. Discount: 6,000 × ₹10 = ₹60,000. After write-off: SPR Dr. ₹60,000 → Discount Cr. ₹60,000. Net: SPR = Nil; Discount = Nil. Wait — option A shows SPR ₹30,000 and Discount Nil, which doesn't match. Let me re-examine: SPR ₹60,000; Discount ₹60,000. Full write-off: both = Nil. But options don't show both nil. Option C shows SPR Nil; Discount Nil — that's the correct net result. Option C.

questions.question: Blaze Infrastructure issued 50,000, 8% Debentures of ₹100 each at ₹92 for cash on 1st April 2023. Year closes 31st March. Discount to be written off over 8 years, using SPR (₹1,50,000 available). First 3 years use SPR; remainder from P&L. P&L charge per year from Year 4 onwards:
questions.explanation: Total discount = 50,000 × ₹8 = ₹4,00,000. Annual write-off = ₹4,00,000 ÷ 8 = ₹50,000. SPR available = ₹1,50,000. Years 1-3: ₹50,000/yr × 3 = ₹1,50,000 from SPR (exhausted). Remaining discount after 3 years = ₹4,00,000 − ₹1,50,000 = ₹2,50,000. Remaining years = 8 − 3 = 5. But annual installment is ₹50,000 (fixed 8-year write-off). So Year 4 onwards: ₹50,000/year from P&L. Total from P&L = 5 × ₹50,000 = ₹2,50,000. P&L charge from Year 4 = ₹50,000. Option A.

questions.question: Magnum Ltd.'s Trial Balance shows: 10% Debentures ₹25,00,000 (Cr.); Bank ₹3,75,000 (Cr. under Debentureholders A/c = interest due); Discount on Issue ₹75,000 (Dr.). During year: (a) Pays debenture interest due; (b) Writes off ₹25,000 discount against SPR ₹1,00,000. P&L impact of debenture-related items for the year, and closing SPR balance:
questions.explanation: Interest already due (shown in trial balance) = ₹3,75,000 — this was already expensed in prior year when accrued. Payment this year: Bank Dr. ₹3,75,000 → Debentureholders Cr. Nil P&L impact from payment (already accrued). But if accrual happens this year: P&L Dr. ₹3,75,000. Also, in current year, new interest accrual may arise. For the discount write-off: SPR Dr. ₹25,000 → Discount Cr. ₹25,000 — no P&L impact. Closing SPR = ₹1,00,000 − ₹25,000 = ₹75,000. P&L impact = ₹3,75,000 (interest); Discount write-off through SPR = Nil to P&L. Option B: P&L ₹3,75,000 + Nil discount; SPR ₹75,000.
